user_query: "A police department is asking us for a student's records. Do we have to provide them?"
statutory_provision:
20 U.S.C. § 1232g(b)(1)(E) No funds shall be made available under any applicable program to any educational agency or institution which has a policy or practice of permitting the release of education records (or personally identifiable information contained therein other than directory information, as defined in paragraph (5) of subsection (a)) of students without the written consent of their parents to any individual, agency, or organization, other than to the following —
(E): State and local officials or authorities to whom such information is specifically allowed to be reported or disclosed pursuant to State statute adopted — (i) before November 19, 1974, if the allowed reporting or disclosure concerns the juvenile justice system and such system's ability to effectively serve the student whose records are released; or (ii) after November 19, 1974, if — (I) the allowed reporting or disclosure concerns the juvenile justice system and such system's ability to effectively serve, before adjudication, the student whose records are released; and (II) the officials and authorities to whom such information is disclosed certify in writing to the educational agency or institution that the information will not be disclosed to any other party except as provided under State law without the prior written consent of the parent of the student.
20 U.S.C. § 1232g(b)(1)(J) Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to prohibit educational agencies or institutions from disclosing education records to a court in compliance with a lawfully issued subpoena, provided the educational agency or institution makes a reasonable effort to notify the parent or eligible student of the order or subpoena in advance of compliance.
expected_followup:
What is the legal basis for the request?  Is the police department presenting a court order or subpoena, requesting records under a state juvenile justice statute, or making an informal request?
A state juvenile justice statute may authorise disclosure, but its date of adoption determines which conditions apply: pre-1974 statutes carry fewer requirements than post-1974 ones. An informal request with no legal basis does not authorise disclosure without consent. The model cannot provide correct advice without knowing which situation applies.
query_continuation: "The police department has not presented a subpoena or court order. They say they are investigating a juvenile matter involving the student and believe the records are relevant.”
expected_final_answer: Without a subpoena, court order, or applicable state juvenile justice statute specifically authorising disclosure, FERPA does not permit the LEA to release the student's records to the police department based on an informal request. The general rule under FERPA is that disclosure without written parental consent is prohibited. The juvenile justice exception under §1232g(b)(1)(E) applies only where a state statute specifically authorizes the disclosure and the state statute's date of adoption determines which conditions must be met. For statutes adopted before November 19, 1974, the disclosure must concern the juvenile justice system's ability to serve the student effectively. For statutes adopted after November 19, 1974, two additional conditions apply: the disclosure must concern the system's ability to serve the student before adjudication, and the receiving officials must provide a written certification to the LEA that the information will not be further disclosed except as provided under state law without prior written parental consent. None of these conditions is triggered by an informal police request. The LEA should decline and advise the police department to obtain a subpoena or identify the specific state statute authorising disclosure, at which point the LEA can assess whether the applicable conditions are satisfied.

user_query: "We are a state education agency. We use a third-party vendor to operate our student data platform. The vendor's engineers need access to individually identifiable student records to perform maintenance and troubleshoot a technical issue. We do not have parental consent for this access. Are we in compliance with FERPA?"
statutory_provision:
20 U.S.C. § 1232g(b)(1)(A)
Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to prohibit educational agencies or institutions from disclosing education records to other school officials, including teachers, within the agency or institution determined to have legitimate educational interests, including an educational agency or institution that has contracted with an outside organization for any institutional service or function for which the agency or institution would otherwise use its own employees, provided that such outside organization is subject to the requirements of this section governing use and redisclosure of personally identifiable information from education records.
expected_followup:
What do the SEA's contracts with the vendor say about the vendor's obligations regarding student data, specifically whether the vendor is contractually bound by FERPA requirements equivalent to those applicable to the SEA's own employees?
The school official exception under §1232g(b)(1)(A) permits disclosure to outside contractors only if three conditions are met: the contractor performs an institutional service or function, is under the direct control of the agency with respect to the records, and is subject to the same FERPA conditions as agency employees. The contract terms are the legally determinative fact.
query_continuation: "Our contract with the vendor designates them as a contractor, prohibits them from using student data for any purpose other than providing services to the SEA, and requires them to comply with FERPA. The contract does not, however, limit which individual engineers within the vendor company can access the data."
expected_final_answer: The SEA is likely in compliance with FERPA since the vendor contract satisfies the main conditions of the school official exception under §1232g(b)(1)(A): the vendor is performing an institutional service, is contractually bound by FERPA requirements, and is prohibited from using data for other purposes. However, the absence of a contractual limitation on which individual engineers can access the data is a meaningful compliance risk. FERPA's legitimate educational interest requirement implies that access should be limited to those with a genuine need for it in connection with the contracted service. The SEA should consider amending the contract to require the vendor to limit internal access to those engineers directly involved in maintaining or supporting the platform, and to maintain an access log.

A university’s campus police department maintains two types of incident reports:
Reports created solely for law enforcement purposes (e.g., criminal investigation of theft in dorms), stored in the police database;
Reports that document student behavior violations for the university’s disciplinary office, sometimes overlapping with the criminal investigation.
A parent requests access to an incident report concerning their 19-year-old child, claiming they want to understand the disciplinary outcome. The campus police argue that the report is protected under FERPA because it contains the student’s education record, while the registrar believes the parent may have rights to see it under directory/record disclosure provisions.

We need to determine: Whether FERPA allows disclosure to the parent, and How to distinguish which portions of the record may be released?
expected_followup: Can you clarify whether the incident report was created primarily for law enforcement purposes or primarily for institutional disciplinary purposes?
query_continuation:
The incident report contains:
Timeline of events, witness statements, and security camera images collected by campus police for a criminal investigation.
A short summary noting that the student may have violated the campus code of conduct, which will be referred to the disciplinary office.
The parent requests access to the full report, including witness statements and security footage.
expected_final_answer:
FERPA Scope & Law Enforcement Exemption: Records created and maintained by a law enforcement unit for law enforcement purposes are not education records under §1232g(a)(4)(B)(ii). Such records cannot be disclosed to parents, even if they contain personally identifiable information about students.
Disciplinary Portion: The portion of the record that summarizes alleged student misconduct referred to the disciplinary office is part of the education record. If the student is under 21 and the violation involves alcohol or controlled substances, the parent may have conditional access under §1232g(i). Otherwise, parental access requires student consent. The university must segregate law enforcement content (investigative notes, evidence, witness identities) from disciplinary summaries. Only the disciplinary portion may be disclosed if FERPA conditions are met; the investigative law enforcement portion must remain confidential.

A national scholarship foundation has asked us to send them an enrolled student’s academic transcript, disciplinary record, and a copy of the student’s most recent financial aid file. We do not have the student’s written consent. May we share the records under FERPA?
statutory_provision:
(b)(1)(D)  No funds shall be made available under any applicable program to any educational agency or institution which has a policy or practice of permitting the release of education records (or personally identifiable information contained therein other than directory information, as defined in paragraph (5) of subsection (a)) of students without the written consent of their parents to any individual, agency, or organization, other than to the following— in connection with a student’s application for, or receipt of, financial aid.
(b)(4)(B)  With respect to this subsection, personal information shall only be transferred to a third party on the condition that such party will not permit any other party to have access to such information without the written consent of the parents of the student. If a third party outside the educational agency or institution permits access to information in violation of paragraph (2)(A), or fails to destroy information in violation of paragraph (1)(F), the educational agency or institution shall be prohibited from permitting access to information from education records to that third party for a period of not less than five years.

The financial aid file was requested so the foundation can coordinate the scholarship with other awards the student is receiving.

The transcript and disciplinary record are reasonably related to eligibility and conditions, and the financial aid file is reasonably related to coordination of awards, so these disclosures fall within the exception.
Two important caveats apply. First, the exception is purpose-limited: you should disclose only the information that is actually necessary for the foundation’s eligibility, amount, conditions, or enforcement determinations, not the student’s entire file. Second, under (b)(4)(B), the foundation as a third-party recipient is prohibited from redisclosing the information to any other party without the written consent of the student. If they fail to comply, they must not be permitted to access information from your institution’s education records, for a period of not less than five years.

user_query: I am the director of a university counseling center. A 21-year-old enrolled student has submitted a written FERPA request to inspect “all records the university maintains about me,” and has specifically asked for the session notes from her appointments with one of our staff psychologists. Are we required under FERPA to give the student access to those notes?
statutory_provision: (a)(4)(B)(iv)  The term “education records” does not include—records on a student who is eighteen years of age or older, or is attending an institution of postsecondary education, which are made or maintained by a physician, psychiatrist, psychologist, or other recognized professional or paraprofessional acting in his professional or paraprofessional capacity, or assisting in that capacity, and which are made, maintained, or used only in connection with the provision of treatment to the student, and are not available to anyone other than persons providing such treatment, except that such records can be personally reviewed by a physician or other appropriate professional of the student’s choice.
expected_followup: Are the counseling notes made and maintained solely in connection with the provision of treatment to the student, kept separate from the university’s general education records, and inaccessible to anyone outside the treating clinicians (and any substitute treating professionals)?
query_continuation: Yes. The notes are created and used only for the purpose of providing mental health treatment to the student. They are not used by any other office of the university for any other purpose.
expected_final_answer:
No, you are not required under FERPA to give the student direct access to the counseling notes, because under those facts the notes are not “education records” within the meaning of FERPA at all. Section (a)(4)(B)(iv) excludes from the definition of education records any records on a student who is eighteen or older, or attending a postsecondary institution, that are made or maintained by a physician, psychiatrist, psychologist, or similar professional, that are used only in connection with treatment, and that are not available to anyone other than the treating professionals. Because the notes you describe satisfy all three conditions, FERPA’s right of inspection and review does not reach them.
There is, however, an important caveat built into the same provision: although the student does not have a direct right of access under FERPA, the treatment records “can be personally reviewed by a physician or other appropriate professional of the student’s choice.” If the student designates an outside clinician to review the notes on her behalf, you must make the notes available for that review.

user_query: "I'm a school counselor at a middle school. I keep a personal notebook with my own observations about students I meet with — reminders to follow up, impressions about a student's mood, notes that help me prepare for the next session. A parent has formally requested under FERPA to inspect 'all records' the school has on her child. Do I have to give her access to my notebook?"
statutory_provision:
20 U.S.C. § 1232g(a)(4)(A) For the purposes of this section, the term "education records" means … those records, files, documents, and other materials which — (i) contain information directly related to a student; and (ii) are maintained by an educational agency or institution or by a person acting for such agency or institution.

20 U.S.C. § 1232g(a)(4)(B) The term "education records" does not include — (i) records of instructional, supervisory, and administrative personnel and educational personnel ancillary thereto which are in the sole possession of the maker thereof and which are not accessible or revealed to any other person except a substitute …
expected_followup:
Has any portion of the notebook ever been shared with, shown to, or revealed to any other person (other than a substitute counselor) — and have any of its contents been transcribed or transferred into any record maintained by the school?

The "sole possession" exclusion in (a)(4)(B)(i) is narrow. It applies only as long as the notes have remained exclusively in the maker's possession and have never been used as the basis for an entry in any school-maintained record. Sharing with a colleague, an administrator, or another school official forfeits the exclusion. Using the notes to write entries into a school-maintained system does not retroactively void the exclusion as to the original notes, but it does mean that the maintained entries are themselves education records subject to inspection.
query_continuation: "The notebook has been kept entirely in my locked desk drawer. I have never shared its contents with anyone. However, I do consult my notes when writing entries into the school's official student behavior tracking system, which is maintained district-wide and viewable by other staff."
expected_final_answer:
You are not required to provide the parent access to the notebook itself. Because the notebook has remained in your sole possession and has never been revealed to any other person (other than a substitute, none of which has occurred here), it falls within the §1232g(a)(4)(B)(i) "sole possession" exclusion and is not an "education record" under FERPA. The parent's right of access does not reach it.

The entries you have made into the district's behavior tracking system, however, are "education records" within (a)(4)(A) — they are directly related to the student and are maintained by the district. The parent has the right to inspect those entries within a reasonable period of time, but no later than 45 days after the request, under §1232g(a)(1)(A).

If you were ever to share, transmit, or reveal the notebook's contents to another person — even informally — the affected portions would lose the sole-possession protection and would become education records subject to inspection from that point forward.

user_query: "We are a state education agency. Two years ago we disclosed individually identifiable student records to a research vendor under FERPA's studies exception. We have just learned that the lead researcher posted a dataset online that allowed re-identification of several students, and that the vendor never destroyed the records on the schedule the agreement required. What are our obligations now under FERPA?"
statutory_provision: 20 U.S.C. § 1232g(b)(4)(B) With respect to this subsection, personal information shall only be transferred to a third party on the condition that such party will not permit any other party to have access to such information without the written consent of the parents of the student. If a third party outside the educational agency or institution permits access to information in violation of paragraph (2)(A), or fails to destroy information in violation of paragraph (1)(F), the educational agency or institution shall be prohibited from permitting access to information from education records to that third party for a period of not less than five years.
expected_followup:
Has the third party (a) permitted access to the information by a non-authorized party without parental consent, in violation of paragraph (2)(A); (b) failed to destroy the information when no longer needed, in violation of paragraph (1)(F); or (c) both?

The five-year ban in (b)(4)(B) is triggered specifically by violations of (2)(A) (unauthorized further disclosure) or (1)(F) (failure to destroy when no longer needed). Either trigger is sufficient. The statutory consequence — at least a five-year prohibition on further sharing with that recipient — attaches automatically. Other obligations (parent/student notification, contract enforcement, state-law breach response) follow the trigger.
query_continuation: "Both. The researcher's online posting permitted access by parties outside the studies-exception agreement. The data was required to be destroyed six months ago at the conclusion of the study, but the dataset the researcher posted was still in their possession well past that date."
expected_final_answer:
Under §1232g(b)(4)(B), the SEA is now prohibited for at least five years from permitting access to education records to that third-party recipient. The ban attaches as a matter of statutory consequence — both grounds are independently triggered (unauthorized redisclosure under (2)(A) and failure to destroy under (1)(F)). The five-year period is a floor, not a ceiling: the SEA has discretion to extend the ban or to apply it more broadly (for example, to the researcher's institution as a whole, depending on contractual terms and the scope of the violation).

The SEA's additional obligations include: (1) document the violation and the imposition of the ban in its records; (2) enforce contractual remedies under the original studies-exception written agreement (return/destruction of any remaining data, indemnification); (3) notify affected students and parents (FERPA does not by itself require this, but state breach-notification laws likely will); and (4) review whether the violation also implicates state or other federal data-protection regimes.

user_query: "I am the registrar at a four-year college that is in the middle of a regional accreditation review. The accreditation site visit team has asked us to provide samples of student transcripts, advising notes, and individual financial aid files for the visiting committee to review during their on-site visit next month. We do not have student consent. Are we permitted to share?"
statutory_provision: 20 U.S.C. § 1232g(b)(1)(G) Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to prohibit … disclosing education records to … accrediting organizations in order to carry out their accrediting functions.
expected_followup:
Is the requesting body acting in its capacity as an accrediting organization carrying out an accrediting function (e.g., a regional or specialized accreditor performing a periodic accreditation review of the institution), and are the records requested ones the accreditor needs in order to evaluate the institution against its accreditation criteria?

The (b)(1)(G) exception is narrow in two ways. First, the recipient must be an "accrediting organization" (typically a federally recognized regional or programmatic accreditor). Second, the disclosure must be in order to carry out the accreditor's accrediting function — not, for example, a researcher employed by the accreditor doing unrelated work, and not records collected for purposes outside the accreditation review.
query_continuation: "The visiting team is from our regional accreditor (recognized by the U.S. Department of Education) and is performing the institution's ten-year reaccreditation review. The records they have requested are samples they routinely review to verify the integrity of academic advising, registrar processes, and financial-aid administration."
expected_final_answer:
Yes, the institution may disclose the requested records to the accrediting team without student consent under §1232g(b)(1)(G). The visiting team is acting on behalf of a recognized accrediting organization carrying out its accrediting function (the periodic reaccreditation review), and the records requested are reasonably necessary to evaluate the institution's compliance with accreditation standards. The institution should record the disclosure under §1232g(b)(4)(A). The accrediting organization is bound by the redisclosure restrictions of §1232g(b)(4)(B): it may not further disclose the records without consent, and is subject to the five-year ban on further disclosures if it permits unauthorized access. The institution should also limit the disclosure to records reasonably necessary for the accreditation function — providing entire databases or sweeping access beyond the team's stated need would not be supported by the exception.

user_query: "Our middle school has a School Resource Officer assigned by the city police department. He's asking to view a student's education file in connection with an investigation of a fight. Do we need parental consent?"
statutory_provision:
20 U.S.C. § 1232g(b)(1)(A) Nothing in this section shall be construed to prohibit … disclosing education records … to other school officials, including teachers, within the agency or institution determined to have legitimate educational interests, including an educational agency or institution that has contracted with an outside organization for any institutional service or function for which the agency or institution would otherwise use its own employees, provided that such outside organization is subject to the requirements of this section governing use and redisclosure of personally identifiable information from education records.

34 CFR § 99.31(a)(1)(i)(B) A contractor, consultant, volunteer, or other party to whom an agency or institution has outsourced institutional services or functions may be considered a school official under this paragraph provided that the outside party — (1) Performs an institutional service or function for which the agency or institution would otherwise use employees; (2) Is under the direct control of the agency or institution with respect to the use and maintenance of education records; and (3) Is subject to the requirements of § 99.33(a) governing the use and redisclosure of personally identifiable information from education records.
expected_followup:
Has the school's annual FERPA notification specified the SRO (or the role/category of SRO) as a "school official with legitimate educational interest," and does the SRO operate under the school's direct control with respect to use and maintenance of education records?

FERPA permits a school to designate outside parties — including SROs — as school officials, but only if four conditions are satisfied: institutional service/function, direct control, redisclosure restrictions, and inclusion in the annual notice. If the SRO is acting in his or her capacity as a city police officer rather than as a designated school official, the request must instead satisfy a different exception (subpoena, court order, juvenile-justice statute, or emergency).

The SRO says he is investigating the fight as part of his school-safety role, not as a criminal matter."
expected_final_answer:
Yes, this disclosure is permitted without parental consent under §1232g(b)(1)(A) and 34 CFR § 99.31(a)(1)(i)(B). The SRO qualifies as a "school official" with "legitimate educational interest" because all four conditions are met: (1) the school has designated SROs in its annual notice; (2) the city/school agreement places the SRO under direct school control as to use and maintenance of records; (3) the agreement bars unauthorized redisclosure (§ 99.33(a)); and (4) the SRO is performing an institutional school-safety function. The SRO may view records reasonably necessary to the school-safety investigation. If the SRO later wants to use the records for a criminal investigation outside the school's institutional function — for example, sharing them with a prosecutor's office — that is a redisclosure that requires either a separate FERPA exception or written consent, regardless of the SRO's status as a designated school official.

user_query: "I'm the principal of a high school. A student we expelled last year for assaulting another student has just enrolled at a high school in a neighboring district. The receiving principal has asked me whether I can share the student's disciplinary file so that she can plan supervision and supports. Are we permitted to share without parental consent?"
statutory_provision: 20 U.S.C. § 1232g(h) Nothing in this section shall prohibit an educational agency or institution from — (1) including appropriate information in the education record of any student concerning disciplinary action taken against such student for conduct that posed a significant risk to the safety or well-being of that student, other students, or other members of the school community; or (2) disclosing such information to teachers and school officials, including teachers and school officials in other schools, who have legitimate educational interests in the behavior of the student.
expected_followup:
Was the disciplinary action documented in the student's education record at your high school taken for conduct that "posed a significant risk to the safety or well-being" of the student, other students, or other members of the school community? And does the receiving principal have a legitimate educational interest in the student's behavior (for example, for supervisory or safety planning)?

The (h) exception is narrower than the general school-official exception. It applies only to disciplinary information in the record concerning conduct posing a "significant risk to safety or well-being" and authorizes disclosure to teachers and school officials with a legitimate educational interest — explicitly including teachers and school officials in other schools.
query_continuation: "Yes — the expulsion was for an assault that resulted in serious injury to another student. The receiving principal has a clear legitimate educational interest in being aware of this history for supervision and safety-planning purposes."
expected_final_answer:
Yes, the disclosure is permitted under §1232g(h). The disciplinary action — expulsion for an assault causing serious injury — concerns conduct that posed a significant risk to the safety or well-being of other students. The (h)(2) provision specifically authorizes disclosure of such disciplinary information to "teachers and school officials in other schools" who have legitimate educational interests, which the receiving principal does in connection with supervision and safety planning at the new school.

This is in addition to the school-transfer exception under §1232g(b)(1)(B), which would also support transferring the student's full education record to a school where the student is enrolled. The (h) exception is helpful when the disclosure is more limited (a particular incident report rather than the entire record) or when the receiving school official wants a head-start on safety planning before formally requesting the full record.

The disclosure must be recorded under §1232g(b)(4)(A). The receiving principal is bound by §1232g(b)(4)(B) redisclosure restrictions: she may not further disclose without consent or another exception.

user_query: "I'm a dean of students at a private four-year college. We have just learned, through routine processing of state sex-offender registry data, that a newly enrolled student is a registered sex offender. We are considering whether to disclose this status to certain campus units (residential life, athletics, the student's faculty advisor). Does FERPA permit this without the student's consent?"
statutory_provision:
20 U.S.C. § 1232g(b)(7)(A) Nothing in this section may be construed to prohibit an educational institution from disclosing information provided to the institution under section 14071 of title 42 concerning registered sex offenders who are required to register under such section.

20 U.S.C. § 1232g(b)(7)(B) The Secretary shall take appropriate steps to notify educational institutions that disclosure of information described in subparagraph (A) is permitted.
expected_followup:
Was the information about the student's registered-sex-offender status received by the institution through a sex-offender-registry notification under the federal registration scheme (42 U.S.C. § 14071), or did the institution learn of the status through some other means (for example, a student's self-disclosure on an application, a media report, or an informal source)?

The (b)(7) exception is specifically tied to information provided to the institution under the federal sex-offender registration scheme. It does not reach information the institution acquired through other channels. If the source of the information is not a registry notification, the institution must look elsewhere (directory-information disclosure, the safety/health emergency exception, or consent) to authorize disclosure.
query_continuation: "The institution learned of the student's status through a registry notification provided pursuant to the federal sex-offender registration scheme."
expected_final_answer:
Yes, FERPA permits the institution to disclose this information without the student's consent under §1232g(b)(7)(A). Because the information was received through a sex-offender-registry notification under the federal scheme, the (b)(7) exception authorizes disclosure to whom the institution determines disclosure is appropriate. The exception is permissive, not mandatory; institutional policy should govern the scope of internal sharing (for example, limiting to officials with a clear safety- or supervision-related need to know) and the timing and form of any communications. The institution should also coordinate with counsel on Title IX and other anti-discrimination considerations, and follow any applicable state-law restrictions on the use of registry information.

If the institution had learned of the student's status through a non-registry source — for example, the student's self-disclosure on an application — (b)(7) would not authorize disclosure, and the institution would need to find another exception or obtain consent.

user_query: "I'm a dean of admissions at a private university. A current student has asked under FERPA to inspect the confidential letters of recommendation that were submitted in support of his application for admission three years ago. He signed a waiver at the time he applied, waiving his right of access to those letters. Are we required to provide him access?"
statutory_provision:
20 U.S.C. § 1232g(a)(1)(C) The first sentence of subparagraph (A) shall not operate to make available to students in institutions of postsecondary education the following materials: … (iii) if the student has signed a waiver of the student's right of access under this subsection in accordance with subparagraph (D), confidential recommendations — (I) respecting admission to any educational agency or institution, …

20 U.S.C. § 1232g(a)(1)(D) A student or a person applying for admission may waive his right of access to confidential statements described in clause (iii) of subparagraph (C), except that such waiver shall apply to recommendations only if (i) the student is, upon request, notified of the names of all persons making confidential recommendations and (ii) such recommendations are used solely for the purpose for which they were specifically intended. Such waivers may not be required as a condition for admission to, receipt of financial aid from, or receipt of any other services or benefits from such agency or institution.
expected_followup:
Was the student's waiver effective under both conditions of §1232g(a)(1)(D) — specifically, (i) is the student, upon request, notified of the names of all persons making the confidential recommendations, and (ii) have the recommendations been used solely for their intended admissions purpose?

An (a)(1)(C)(iii) waiver applies to recommendations only if both (D) conditions hold. The student must be notified, on request, of the names of recommenders, and the recommendations must have been used only for the purpose for which they were specifically intended (admissions). If either condition fails, the waiver does not bar the student from accessing the recommendations.
query_continuation: "The waiver was signed at the time of application. The student has not previously asked for the names of the recommenders, and the institution has the names available on request. The recommendations were used only in connection with the admissions decision and have not been consulted or shared for any other purpose since."
expected_final_answer:
The institution is not required to provide the student access to the confidential recommendations under FERPA. The student's waiver under §1232g(a)(1)(D) operates to bar his right of access to the confidential admissions recommendations described in (a)(1)(C)(iii), and both conditions of (D) appear satisfied: the institution will, upon request, notify the student of the names of the persons who provided the recommendations (you should provide that list now if he asks), and the recommendations have been used solely for the admissions purpose for which they were intended.

If either condition were violated — for example, if the institution had used the recommendation for some unrelated purpose, or if it refused to identify the recommenders on request — the waiver would not apply and the student would have the right of access. The institution should confirm both conditions are still satisfied before declining the request.

user_query: "A parent has signed and submitted a one-line written consent that says: 'I consent to the school sharing my child's records as needed.' Based on this, the school registrar is preparing to release the student's full education file to a private tutoring company that the parent contracts with. Is this consent adequate under FERPA?"
statutory_provision: 20 U.S.C. § 1232g(b)(2)(A) No funds shall be made available under any applicable program to any educational agency or institution which has a policy or practice of releasing, or providing access to, any personally identifiable information in education records other than directory information, or as is permitted under paragraph (1) of this subsection, unless — there is written consent from the student's parents specifying records to be released, the reasons for such release, and to whom, and with a copy of the records to be released to the student's parents and the student if desired by the parents.
expected_followup:
Does the parent's written consent form specify (i) which records are to be released, (ii) the reasons for the release, and (iii) to whom the records are to be released?

A FERPA consent must be in writing and specify three elements: the records, the reason, and the recipient. A general or open-ended consent ("share as needed") does not satisfy any of these. The institution may not rely on such a consent to authorize disclosure.
query_continuation: "The form contains only the one line quoted above. It does not name the tutoring company, does not list any specific records, and does not state a reason."
expected_final_answer:
The consent is not adequate under FERPA, and the school may not release the records on the basis of this form. §1232g(b)(2)(A) requires written consent that specifies three things: (i) the records to be released, (ii) the reasons for the release, and (iii) to whom the records are to be released. The parent's one-line, open-ended consent fails all three. The school should return the form to the parent, explain the requirements, and ask the parent to complete a sufficient consent form (most schools provide a template). If the parent then provides a compliant consent — for example, "I consent to the release of my child's grade reports and attendance records, for the purpose of supporting after-school tutoring, to ABC Tutoring Co." — the school may release only the records specified, only for the stated purpose, and only to the named recipient.

user_query: "Our high school has a threat assessment team that includes our school counselor and dean, plus a community mental-health clinician and a sergeant from the city police. The team needs to review the records of a student who has been making concerning statements. Are we allowed to share with the outside members?"
statutory_provision:
34 CFR § 99.31(a)(1)(i)(B) A contractor, consultant, volunteer, or other party to whom an agency or institution has outsourced institutional services or functions may be considered a school official under this paragraph provided that the outside party — (1) Performs an institutional service or function for which the agency or institution would otherwise use employees; (2) Is under the direct control of the agency or institution with respect to the use and maintenance of education records; and (3) Is subject to the requirements of § 99.33(a) governing the use and redisclosure of personally identifiable information from education records.

34 CFR § 99.31(a)(1)(i)(A) A school official has a legitimate educational interest if the official needs to review an education record in order to fulfill his or her professional responsibility.
expected_followup:
Are the outside threat-assessment team members designated as "school officials with legitimate educational interest" under the school's annual FERPA notification, and is the school's agreement with each outside member sufficient to place the member under the school's direct control and to bind them to FERPA's redisclosure restrictions?

Threat assessment is a permissible institutional function for which the school could use its own employees; outside members can therefore be designated as school officials, but only if the four conditions of § 99.31(a)(1)(i)(B) are met for each member. Without a designation and an appropriate agreement, sharing PII with the outside members is not authorized under the school-official exception.
query_continuation: "The school district's annual FERPA notice describes the threat assessment team and identifies its categories of members. The district has a memorandum of understanding with both the community mental-health agency and the police department that places those individuals under the district's direct control with respect to use and maintenance of education records and that prohibits redisclosure. The records to be reviewed are necessary to the team's professional responsibility to assess the threat."
expected_final_answer:
Yes, the school may share the relevant education records with all team members, including the community mental-health clinician and the police sergeant, without parental consent. Each outside member qualifies as a "school official with legitimate educational interest" under 34 CFR § 99.31(a)(1)(i)(B): the threat assessment function is one the school would otherwise perform with employees; the MOUs place each member under the district's direct control; and the MOUs prohibit redisclosure consistent with § 99.33(a). The annual notification has identified the categories of school officials, satisfying the fourth condition. The records shared should be those necessary to the team's professional responsibility — not the student's full education record. If the police sergeant later seeks to use information learned from the team for a criminal investigation, that is a separate redisclosure and must be authorized by the district or supported by another exception. Disclosures under this exception are not required to be logged in the student's file (34 CFR § 99.32(d)(1)).

user_query: "A parent has asked to see a school-bus surveillance video that shows their child being involved in an altercation with two other students. The school used the video to discipline all three. Are we required to let this parent view it?"
statutory_provision:
20 U.S.C. § 1232g(a)(1)(A) No funds shall be made available under any applicable program to any educational agency or institution which has a policy of denying, or which effectively prevents, the parents of students … the right to inspect and review the education records of their children.

34 CFR § 99.12(a) If the education records of a student contain information on more than one student, the parent or eligible student may inspect and review or be informed of only the specific information about that student.
expected_followup:
Does the video qualify as an education record of the requesting parent's child (i.e., is it directly related to the child and maintained by the school), and can the video be redacted or segregated in a way that allows the parent to view only the portions related to their child without destroying the meaning of the record?

A surveillance video that the school maintains and uses to discipline a student is "directly related to" that student and is therefore an education record of that student. When the same record is the education record of multiple students, FERPA requires the school to allow the requesting parent to inspect/review the portion related to their child or to be "informed of" the content. The dispositive question is whether redaction or segregation is reasonably feasible.
query_continuation: "The video clearly shows all three students throughout the relevant scene; their faces and identifying features are simultaneously visible. Redaction (e.g., blurring) is technically possible but would require a third-party vendor; segregation (extracting frames showing only the requesting parent's child) is not feasible because the children are continually together in frame."
expected_final_answer:
The school must allow the parent to inspect and review the video, but the school has discretion in how it satisfies that obligation. Under 34 CFR § 99.12(a) and the Department's guidance on photos and videos, the parent has the right to inspect/review only the information related to their own child. Where redaction or segregation can be reasonably accomplished without destroying the meaning of the record, the school must do so and may not charge the parent for that work. Where redaction is not reasonably feasible — as here, because the students are continually together in frame and segregation is impossible — the school may permit the parent to inspect/review the unredacted video, since the alternative would be to "effectively prevent" the parent from inspecting their child's record in violation of §1232g(a)(1)(A). If the school chooses, instead, to "inform" the parent of the content (e.g., a written description) rather than display the video, that also satisfies FERPA's inspection right. FERPA does not require the school to provide a copy of the video. The school may also wish to consider whether the other students' parents need to be informed.

user_query: "A second-grade teacher observed a student behaving erratically at recess. The teacher mentioned the incident to a parent volunteer who was supervising. The student's parent has now complained that the teacher disclosed information about their child without consent. Did we violate FERPA?"
statutory_provision:
20 U.S.C. § 1232g(a)(4)(A) For the purposes of this section, the term "education records" means … those records, files, documents, and other materials which — (i) contain information directly related to a student; and (ii) are maintained by an educational agency or institution or by a person acting for such agency or institution.

34 CFR § 99.3 (definition of "education records") The term … does not include … (5) Records that only contain information about an individual after he or she is no longer a student at that agency or institution.
expected_followup:
Did the teacher's communication convey information that originated in or is documented in an education record maintained by the school, or did it convey only what the teacher personally observed and which is not reflected in any written or otherwise-maintained record?

FERPA governs disclosures of personally identifiable information from education records. Information based purely on a school official's personal knowledge or observation — never reduced to a "maintained" record — is not within FERPA's scope. The fact that the observation occurred in the teacher's official role does not, by itself, transform it into an education record.
query_continuation: "The teacher had not yet documented the incident anywhere; the conversation reflected only what the teacher had just personally observed. A behavioral referral form was created later that afternoon, after the conversation."
expected_final_answer:
No FERPA violation occurred. At the time of the conversation, the information communicated was based purely on the teacher's personal observation and was not yet contained in any education record maintained by the school. FERPA applies to "education records," which are records containing information directly related to a student that are maintained by the school or someone acting for it. Personal knowledge that has not been reduced to or recorded in a maintained record is outside FERPA's scope. The subsequent creation of a behavioral referral form does not retroactively transform the earlier oral communication into a FERPA disclosure. (The teacher's later disclosure of the contents of the referral form would be a different question and would fall under FERPA.) The parent may have legitimate professional or pedagogical concerns about the conversation occurring in front of a parent volunteer, but those are not FERPA concerns.

Concepts in explanation

- SCOPE LIMITER: FERPA applies only to "education records" — content must be both directly related to the student and maintained by the school.
- TIME-OF-DISCLOSURE TEST: The test is applied at the moment of communication.

user_query: "My district forgot to send the annual FERPA notification this school year. We're now eight months into the year and a parent has filed a complaint. What does that mean for us?"
statutory_provision:
20 U.S.C. § 1232g(e) No funds shall be made available under any applicable program to any educational agency or institution unless the recipient of such funds informs the parents of students, or the students, if they are eligible students, of the rights accorded them by this section.

34 CFR § 99.7(a)(1) An educational agency or institution shall annually notify parents of students currently in attendance, or eligible students currently in attendance, of their rights under the Act and this part.

34 CFR § 99.7(b) An educational agency or institution may provide this notice by any means that are reasonably likely to inform the parents or eligible students of their rights.
expected_followup:
Did the district provide notice of FERPA rights through any means during the current school year — for example, in a student handbook, parent portal, posted policy, registration packet, or website notice — even if not in the form ordinarily used?

The annual notification requirement is satisfied by any means reasonably likely to inform; the regulation does not require a particular medium. A complete failure to provide any notice is a clear violation; a notice that was provided through a non-traditional channel may satisfy the requirement.
query_continuation: "Our standard practice has been to mail an annual notice in September. We did not do that this year. However, the district website has a permanent FERPA-rights page accessible from the homepage, the parent portal includes a link to that page on first login, and the student handbook (distributed at the start of the year) reproduces the FERPA-rights text in full."
expected_final_answer:
The district has likely satisfied the annual notification requirement, even though it did not send the customary mailing. Under 34 CFR § 99.7(b), notice may be given "by any means … reasonably likely to inform" the parents of their rights. The combination of (1) a permanent website page, (2) a portal link presented on first login, and (3) the FERPA-rights text reproduced in full in the student handbook distributed to all parents at the start of the year is reasonably likely to inform parents of their rights. The Department's enforcement practice has been to focus on whether parents had a meaningful opportunity to learn of their rights, not on whether the district used a particular method. The complaining parent's grievance is unlikely to result in an adverse determination on its face, though the district should restore its mailing as a defensive practice. If the district had no alternative notice channel — no website page, no handbook, no portal — the answer would change and a violation finding would be likely.

user_query: "We received a parent's request to amend her child's permanent record because she believes a noted disciplinary infraction is misleading. After review, we declined to make the change. The parent is now demanding a 'FERPA hearing.' We've never held one. What does FERPA require us to provide?"
statutory_provision: 20 U.S.C. § 1232g(a)(2) No funds shall be made available under any applicable program to any educational agency or institution unless the parents of students who are or have been in attendance at a school of such agency or at such institution are provided an opportunity for a hearing by such agency or institution, in accordance with regulations of the Secretary, to challenge the content of such student's education records, in order to insure that the records are not inaccurate, misleading, or otherwise in violation of the privacy rights of students, and to provide an opportunity for the correction or deletion of any such inaccurate, misleading or otherwise inappropriate data contained therein and to insert into such records a written explanation of the parents respecting the content of such records.
expected_followup:
Is the parent challenging the record on the ground that it is inaccurate, misleading, or in violation of the student's privacy rights — and is she requesting a hearing to challenge that content (rather than, for example, asking the school to reconsider as a matter of policy)?

FERPA's hearing right under (a)(2) is conditional on the challenge being to the content of an education record on grounds that the record is inaccurate, misleading, or in violation of the student's privacy rights. If the parent's challenge meets that scope, the institution must offer a hearing. The hearing must give the parent an opportunity to challenge the content; if the institution declines to amend, the parent has the right to insert a written explanation in the record.
query_continuation: "The parent's letter argues the disciplinary infraction note is misleading because it omits relevant context that would explain her child's behavior. She is asking specifically for a hearing under FERPA."
expected_final_answer:
Yes, the school must provide an opportunity for a hearing under §1232g(a)(2). The parent's challenge is on a permitted ground (that the record is misleading). The hearing must be conducted in accordance with regulations of the Secretary (34 CFR § 99.22 specifies that the hearing must be held within a reasonable time after the request, that the parent be given reasonable advance notice, that the hearing be conducted by a school official who does not have a direct interest in the outcome, that the parent be given a full and fair opportunity to present evidence, that the decision be in writing within a reasonable time of the hearing, that it be based solely on evidence presented at the hearing, and that it include a summary of the evidence and reasons for the decision).

If after the hearing the school still declines to amend, the parent has the right under (a)(2) to insert into the record a written explanation respecting the content. That explanation must be maintained with the contested portion of the record and must be disclosed whenever the contested portion is disclosed to a third party.

The school should formalize a hearing procedure now if it does not have one — many institutions adopt a written policy specifying who serves as the hearing officer, the timeline, and the form of the written decision.

FERPA's amendment right is limited to records that are "inaccurate, misleading, or in violation of the privacy rights of the student." A challenge to a teacher's evaluation of work quality is outside FERPA's scope.

The school must, however, provide the parent with a hearing under § 99.22 if requested, even though the hearing is unlikely to result in amendment, and must allow the parent to insert a written statement of the parent's view into the record under § 99.21(b)(2). Concepts in explanation

- SCOPE LIMITER: FERPA's amendment right does not extend to substantive academic judgments.
- PROCEDURAL OBLIGATION: Even when the school will not amend, it must offer a hearing and allow a written statement from the parent.

user_query: "I'm an admissions counselor at a community college. A person applied to our institution last year, was admitted, but never registered for classes or paid tuition. He has now sent us a written request under FERPA demanding to inspect his entire admissions file, including the readers' notes and his application essays. Is FERPA applicable, and do we have to provide him access?"
statutory_provision: 20 U.S.C. § 1232g(a)(6) For the purposes of this section, the term "student" includes any person with respect to whom an educational agency or institution maintains education records or personally identifiable information, but does not include a person who has not been in attendance at such agency or institution.
expected_followup:
Did the person ever attend the institution — that is, did he register for, attend, or otherwise commence enrollment in any course or program — or did he only apply (and possibly accept admission) without ever enrolling?

The (a)(6) definition of "student" carves out a person who has not been in attendance at the institution. FERPA's rights of access and consent apply to "students" as defined. A person who applied but never attended is not a "student" under FERPA, and FERPA confers no right of access on him. State open-records or institutional-policy obligations may operate independently.
query_continuation: "He was admitted but never registered for any course, never attended any class, never paid tuition. The only record we maintain on him is his admissions file."
expected_final_answer:
FERPA does not apply to this person. Under §1232g(a)(6), the definition of "student" excludes a person who has not been in attendance at the institution. Because he applied and was admitted but never attended (never registered, never paid, never participated in any course), he is not a "student" under FERPA, and the rights and protections that flow from FERPA — including the right to inspect and review education records — do not extend to him.

The institution may decline the request as a matter of FERPA. Two caveats: (1) institutional policy or state law (open-records, consumer-protection, application-fee transparency) may impose independent obligations. (2) If the person were to subsequently enroll, FERPA's protections would attach to records the institution maintains on him from that point forward — and to records of his earlier application that the institution continues to maintain after enrollment.

user_query: "A documentary filmmaker has asked our college for the academic records of a former student who died last year at age 24. They say their work concerns the student's posthumous reputation. Are we permitted to share?"
statutory_provision:
FPCO/PTAC Guidance (informed by 20 U.S.C. § 1232g(d) and common law principles): Once an eligible student dies, FERPA rights lapse. FERPA does not protect the education records of a deceased eligible student, and the institution may disclose such records at its discretion or consistent with State law.

34 CFR § 99.5(a)(1) When a student becomes an eligible student, the rights accorded to, and consent required of, parents under this part transfer from the parents to the student.
expected_followup:
Was the student an "eligible student" under FERPA at the time of death — that is, age 18 or older, or attending a postsecondary institution at any age — or did the student die while a parent still held FERPA rights (under 18 and not in postsecondary)?

The Department's interpretation is that FERPA rights of an eligible student lapse on death; FERPA no longer protects the records, and the institution has discretion to disclose subject to State law. If the student died before becoming an eligible student, the analysis is more complicated and the parent's FERPA rights may continue or transfer at common law; State law and institutional policy then govern.
query_continuation: "The student was 24 at the time of death and had been an eligible student for years. State law has no specific provision governing post-mortem disclosure of education records."
expected_final_answer:
FERPA does not protect the records of this deceased eligible student. The institution may disclose the records at its discretion, subject to its own institutional policy and any applicable State law (here, none). Most institutions adopt a written policy governing post-mortem disclosure — typically requiring approval at a senior level, defining categories of permitted recipients (next of kin, executors, researchers under specified conditions), and considering the student's known wishes if any. The institution should consult its own policy before disclosing to a documentary filmmaker; FERPA is no longer an obstacle, but policy and reputational considerations remain. If the student had been a minor at death (and not enrolled in a postsecondary institution), the analysis would differ and parental consent considerations would persist.

user_query: "I'm an elementary-school principal. A student's parent has discovered that a photograph of her child, taken during the school day by a school-employed photographer for an in-house yearbook, was included on the school's public-facing website. The parent is asserting that her child's photo is an 'education record' under FERPA and that the school should not have published it without consent. Is the parent right that this photo is an education record, and is the publication a FERPA violation?"
statutory_provision:
20 U.S.C. § 1232g(a)(4)(A) The term "education records" means … those records, files, documents, and other materials which — (i) contain information directly related to a student; and (ii) are maintained by an educational agency or institution or by a person acting for such agency or institution.

20 U.S.C. § 1232g(a)(5)(A) For the purposes of this section the term "directory information" relating to a student includes the following: the student's name, address, telephone listing, date and place of birth, major field of study, participation in officially recognized activities and sports, weight and height of members of athletic teams, dates of attendance, degrees and awards received, and the most recent previous educational agency or institution attended by the student.

20 U.S.C. § 1232g(a)(5)(B) Any educational agency or institution making public directory information shall give public notice of the categories of information which it has designated as such information with respect to each student attending the institution or agency and shall allow a reasonable period of time after such notice has been given for a parent to inform the institution or agency that any or all of the information designated should not be released without the parent's prior consent.
expected_followup:
Has the school designated student photographs (or images) as "directory information" in its annual notification, and was the parent given a reasonable opportunity to opt out of the disclosure before the photo was published?

A photograph of a student that the school maintains is an education record under (a)(4)(A). Whether the school may publicly publish the photograph without consent depends on whether photographs are designated as "directory information" and whether the parent had an opportunity to opt out. (a)(5) authorizes public release of directory information without parental consent only after notice and an opportunity to opt out. Without that designation and opt-out process, public publication requires consent.
query_continuation: "The school's annual FERPA notice does not mention photographs as a category of directory information. Photographs are not listed anywhere as directory information in any published policy. The parent did not have any opportunity to opt out."
expected_final_answer:
Yes, the photograph is an education record under §1232g(a)(4)(A) — it contains information directly related to the student, and it is maintained by the school (through the school-employed photographer acting for the school). And yes, the school's publication on its public website without the parent's prior consent violates FERPA, because the photograph was not designated as directory information and the parent was not given the (a)(5)(B) opt-out opportunity before the disclosure.

To remediate, the school should: (1) remove the photograph from the public-facing site; (2) determine whether to designate "photographs" or "images of students" as a category of directory information going forward, and if so, update the annual notice and provide the opt-out window before any future publication; (3) document the violation in the parent's FERPA file; and (4) consider whether other photographs of other students were similarly published and address those parallel issues.

user_query: "A teacher wants to assign a third-party online learning platform that requires each student to register an account using their school email and full name. The platform's terms of service include a clause permitting the company to use student data for "service improvement and analytics." Are we permitted to require students to use this platform without parental consent?"
statutory_provision:
34 CFR § 99.31(a)(1)(i)(B) A contractor, consultant, volunteer, or other party to whom an agency or institution has outsourced institutional services or functions may be considered a school official under this paragraph provided that the outside party — (1) Performs an institutional service or function for which the agency or institution would otherwise use employees; (2) Is under the direct control of the agency or institution with respect to the use and maintenance of education records; and (3) Is subject to the requirements of § 99.33(a) governing the use and redisclosure of personally identifiable information from education records.

34 CFR § 99.33(a)(1) An educational agency or institution may disclose personally identifiable information from an education record only on the condition that the party to whom the information is disclosed will not disclose the information to any other party without the prior consent of the parent or eligible student.
expected_followup:
Has the school evaluated the platform's contract or terms of service against the four conditions of the school-official exception — institutional function, direct control, use restriction, redisclosure prohibition — and does the contract preclude the company from using PII from education records for purposes other than performing the contracted service?

A clickwrap terms-of-service that allows the vendor to use student data for "service improvement and analytics" is incompatible with the school-official exception, which requires the vendor to be under the school's direct control and prohibited from redisclosure or use beyond the contracted service.
query_continuation: "The terms of service have not been negotiated. They are presented to each user (or a parent on the user's behalf) as a clickwrap agreement. The "service improvement and analytics" clause permits the company to use de-identified student usage data for product development, but also retains the right to use identifying data internally. The platform performs a service (interactive math practice) that the school would otherwise use teachers to provide."
expected_final_answer:
The school may not assign this platform on the school-official-exception theory under FERPA. The school-official exception under § 99.31(a)(1)(i)(B) requires that the vendor be under the school's direct control as to use and maintenance of education records and prohibited from using PII for purposes beyond the contracted service. A clickwrap "service improvement and analytics" clause that permits internal use of identifying data fails both conditions: the school is not in direct control of how the vendor uses the data, and the vendor is not restricted to the contracted service. To proceed under the school-official exception, the school (not individual teachers, and not students through clickwrap) must negotiate a contract that places the vendor under the school's direct control, prohibits use of identifying data for any purpose other than performing the contracted service, and binds the vendor to FERPA's redisclosure restrictions. Alternatively, the school may proceed with prior written consent of each student's parent (or eligible student), in which case the consent must specify the records to be disclosed, the purpose, and the recipients (34 CFR § 99.30(b)). State student-privacy laws (in many states) impose additional restrictions on online educational services that may further limit the school's options.

Concepts in explanation

- CONDITIONAL EXCEPTION: The school-official exception requires four conditions, each operative as an independent constraint.
- DEFAULT RULE: Without a qualifying exception, disclosure requires prior written consent under § 99.30.
- CONTRACT-LEVEL CONSTRAINT: Vendor terms-of-service language directly determines FERPA compliance.

user_query: "I'm a campus HR director at a public university. A 21-year-old student who works for us as a part-time research assistant has formally requested under FERPA to inspect 'all records' the institution maintains about her. She wants this to include her personnel file (employment application, performance reviews, payroll records). Are we required to provide access to the personnel file under FERPA?"
statutory_provision: 20 U.S.C. § 1232g(a)(4)(B) The term "education records" does not include — … (iii) in the case of persons who are employed by an educational agency or institution but who are not in attendance at such agency or institution, records made and maintained in the normal course of business which relate exclusively to such person in that person's capacity as an employee and are not available for use for any other purpose …
expected_followup:
Is the student employed by the institution (a) entirely separately from her status as a student — for example, an employee who happens to also be enrolled but whose employment is unrelated to her studies — or (b) employed in a capacity that is contingent on or directly tied to her status as a student (a federal-work-study assignment, a graduate teaching/research assistantship that is part of her academic program)?

The (a)(4)(B)(iii) exclusion applies only to records of persons who are employed but who are NOT in attendance at the institution. A student employee — who is, by definition, in attendance — does not fit the carve-out's predicate. Her employment-related records are therefore education records under (a)(4)(A) to the extent they are maintained by the institution and relate to her. Federal guidance and most institutions' practice limit the FERPA-based right of access to records that are "directly related to the student" qua student, and treat purely employment-administrative records (W-2 forms, payroll deductions) as employment records governed by separate employment-law regimes.
query_continuation: "She is enrolled as a degree-seeking student in our undergraduate program. Her position is a federal work-study assignment — her employment is tied to her enrollment, and her work supervisor coordinates with the financial aid office to confirm continued eligibility."
expected_final_answer:
The (a)(4)(B)(iii) exclusion does not apply, because the student is in attendance at the institution and her employment is tied to her enrollment (federal work-study). Records maintained by the institution about her — including those generated in the course of her work-study employment — are subject to FERPA's "education records" framework to the extent they are directly related to her.

Practically, the institution should: (1) provide access to records that are directly related to the student's role as a student (her academic record, her financial aid file including the work-study certification and eligibility documents, evaluations she received in connection with her work-study placement); (2) treat purely administrative employment records (W-2s, tax-withholding forms, payroll deduction authorizations) under applicable employment-law regimes rather than FERPA, since these are "directly related to" her in her capacity as a wage-earner rather than as a student.

If the student were employed in a role entirely independent of her student status (a regular staff job that happens to be filled by someone who also happens to be enrolled), the (a)(4)(B)(iii) exclusion would more clearly apply and her employment records would not be FERPA records — though as a practical matter, an institution that draws a clear line between student-employment and non-student-employment categories may still provide voluntary access for employee-relations reasons.

"She is enrolled as a degree-seeking student in our undergraduate program. Her position is a federal work-study assignment — her employment is tied to her enrollment, and her work supervisor coordinates with the financial aid office to confirm continued eligibility."

The (a)(4)(B)(iii) exclusion does not apply, because the student is in attendance at the institution and her employment is tied to her enrollment (federal work-study). Records maintained by the institution about her — including those generated in the course of her work-study employment — are subject to FERPA's "education records" framework to the extent they are directly related to her.

Practically, the institution should: (1) provide access to records that are directly related to the student's role as a student (her academic record, her financial aid file including the work-study certification and eligibility documents, evaluations she received in connection with her work-study placement); (2) treat purely administrative employment records (W-2s, tax-withholding forms, payroll deduction authorizations) under applicable employment-law regimes rather than FERPA, since these are "directly related to" her in her capacity as a wage-earner rather than as a student.

If the student were employed in a role entirely independent of her student status (a regular staff job that happens to be filled by someone who also happens to be enrolled), the (a)(4)(B)(iii) exclusion would more clearly apply and her employment records would not be FERPA records — though as a practical matter, an institution that draws a clear line between student-employment and non-student-employment categories may still provide voluntary access for employee-relations reasons.

user_query: "We received a federal grand jury subpoena for a student's records. It includes language ordering us not to disclose the existence of the subpoena to anyone, including the student or their parents. Do we have to comply, and what about FERPA's normal parental notice rule?"
statutory_provision:
20 U.S.C. § 1232g(b)(1)(J)(i) Nothing in this section shall be construed to prohibit … disclosing education records to … (J) Federal and State officials and agencies headed by an official listed in subsection (b)(3), and the entities and persons described in subsection (b)(5), to the extent that such disclosure is in furtherance of an audit, evaluation, or enforcement of Federal legal requirements …

20 U.S.C. § 1232g(b)(2)(B) … such information is furnished in compliance with judicial order, or pursuant to any lawfully issued subpoena, upon condition that parents and the students are notified of all such orders or subpoenas in advance of the compliance therewith by the educational institution or agency.

34 CFR § 99.31(a)(9)(ii)(A) Disclosure may be made under § 99.31(a)(9)(i) without parental notice if the disclosure is in compliance with a Federal grand jury subpoena and the court has ordered that the existence or the contents of the subpoena or the information furnished in response to the subpoena not be disclosed.
expected_followup:
Does the subpoena include an explicit order from the issuing court that the existence or contents of the subpoena (or the information furnished in response) must not be disclosed?

A federal grand jury subpoena is one of three categories under § 99.31(a)(9)(ii) that may displace FERPA's parental-notice rule, but only when accompanied by a non-disclosure order. If the subpoena lacks such an order, ordinary parental-notice obligations apply.
query_continuation: "The subpoena is from a federal grand jury and explicitly orders non-disclosure of its existence and contents under 18 U.S.C. § 6 and the issuing court's seal."
expected_final_answer:
Yes, the school must comply with the subpoena and must not notify the parent or student. Under §1232g(b)(2)(B) and 34 CFR § 99.31(a)(9)(ii)(A), a federal grand jury subpoena accompanied by a court non-disclosure order displaces FERPA's ordinary parental-notice rule. The school should produce the records called for by the subpoena and refrain from any disclosure of the subpoena's existence or the disclosure itself. The school is also relieved of the recordkeeping requirement that would otherwise apply: 34 CFR § 99.32(d)(5) excepts disclosures made pursuant to such non-disclosure-ordered subpoenas from the logging obligation. After the non-disclosure order expires or is lifted, the school's confidentiality obligation as to the existence of the subpoena ends, but the records produced remain otherwise subject to FERPA in the recipient's hands.
